segments.s361-g57: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آگاهی here means 'news/information' (the news of you) and سوی من 'toward me' places the reception (من as object). رسید is a simple past 'reached/arrived'. The whole segment is a temporal subordinate clause: 'When your news reached me.'
segments.s361-g58: به‌روز سپیدم is an adverbial time expression 'on my bright/clear day' (سپید with the possessive suffix -م modifies روز). ستاره is the grammatical subject 'a star', and بدید is the past tense 'was seen/appeared' (3rd singular). The verb has no overt personal subject; the clause means 'a star appeared.'
segments.s361-g59: نوشتم is the 1st-person singular past 'I wrote.' The noun phrase یکی نامه دوست‌وار ('one/a certain letter, friend‑like') follows the verb by poetic inversion and means 'a friendly letter' or 'a letter like a friend.' وار is a suffix meaning '-like' or 'in the manner of.'
segments.s361-g60: که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause 'that/for'. The correlative هم ... و هم ... ('both ... and ...') links دوست 'friends' and نیک یار 'good companions'; بودیم is the past 'we were' (1st‑pl). Overall: 'for we were both friends and good companions.'
segments.s361-g61: چو is 'when' introducing a temporal clause. نامه بخوانی uses the present-subjunctive/imperative sense 'you read' (بخوانی, 2nd‑singular) giving the condition 'when you read the letter.' سر و تن is a coordinated object 'head and body,' and بشوی here is an imperative/subjunctive instruction 'wash (yourself)/bathe' directed at the addressee.
segments.s361-g62: فریبنده را is the definite object ('the deceiver' or 'deceptive one' marked by را). نیز 'also/too' adds emphasis ('also do not...'). منمای is a negative imperative meaning 'do not show/reveal (to me)' and روی is 'the face' (the thing not to be shown). Word order places the object phrase before the negative imperative.
segments.s361-g63: مران is most likely a proper name here (address or name of a thing). بند را is the definite object 'the band/chain' (را marking it). از میان is the adverbial 'from the middle/out of the midst', and باز کن is the imperative construction 'open/unfasten' (باز preceding the imperative کن). Together: 'Unbind Maran's band from the middle.'
segments.s361-g64: به شادی is an adverbial phrase 'with joy/joyfully.' می‌روشن is a present plural verbal form 'they shine/are lit' (here descriptive/participial), and آغاز کن is the imperative 'begin.' The clause instructs to 'begin the brightening/illumination with joy.'
segments.s361-g65: گرایدونک is an addressive epithet (vocative/adjective used to call the addressee) meaning someone who utters a small cry; it stands before the verb as a poetic label. بپذیری is a 2nd-person-present/subjunctive/volitive form (you should/you may accept) of پذیرفتن, and از من marks the source ('accept from me'); تو پند is the object phrase 'my advice for you' with تو functioning as the addressee and پند the noun 'advice'.
segments.s361-g66: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; ترکان is the source/agent 'the Turks'. ت + را together form ترا 'to/for you' (here split in the data as suffix + object marker). نیز 'also/too' adds inclusion, and ناید گزند is a traditional predication meaning 'harm does not come' (ناید here = 'does not come/befall'). The clause order (from‑X to‑you also not‑comes harm) is typical older Persian inversion.
segments.s361-g67: زمین کشانی is an adjectival/agentive compound meaning 'those who seize or conquer lands' (زمین 'land' + کشانی derivative of کشاند/کشان). و joins the next noun; ترکان چین reads 'the Turks of China' (ترکان = Turks, چین = China), so the segment lists two groups in apposition.
segments.s361-g68: ت + را again form ترا 'for you' (object/beneficiary). باشد is a volitive/optative 3rd‑person form 'may be / let it be' (classical Persian uses باشد to express a wish or result), این 'this' points to what was just offered, and همچو ایران‌زمین is a simile 'like the land of Iran.' The word order (beneficiary — verb — demonstrative — simile) is characteristic of older Persian poetic syntax.
segments.s361-g69: به تو marks the indirect object 'to you/for you'. بخشم is a 1st‑person volitive/present ('I will give' / 'I grant') of بخشیدن in the sense 'bestow, give' (not 'forgive' here). این بی‌کران گنجها is the direct object 'these boundless treasures' placed after the verb, which is common in Persian when stressing the beneficiary.
segments.s361-g70: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'. آورده‌ام is perfective 'I have brought/collected' (1st person). گرد here functions idiomatically with آوردن (as in گردآوردن) to mean 'together/gathered' or 'brought together', and با رنجها ('with hardships') gives the manner/instrumental — 'with toil'.
segments.s361-g71: نکورنگ is an adjectival epithet 'well‑colored / handsome' describing اسپان (plural of اسب, 'horses'). با سیم و زر 'with silver and gold' specifies how they are adorned; the adjective precedes the noun in the poetic diction but functions as a usual modifier.
segments.s361-g72: به is a preposition 'to/for'. استامها is an archaic/poetic noun here referring to a part of the horse tackle (context implies 'stirrups' or similar equipment) and is treated as the indirect object/recipient of the gems — none of the supplied senses ('I am / I was') fit this lexical use, so its sense is marked unknown. در نشانده is a perfect participle construction 'has placed in' (نشانده = 'placed/seated') with در 'in', and گهر is the direct object 'a gem; jewel' (i.e. gems placed into those fittings).
segments.s361-g73: غلامان (plural) appears before the verb as the direct objects 'servants'; فرستمت is a 1st‑person past/perfective verb-form meaning 'I sent (to you)' with the clitic -ت marking 'you' as indirect object; با + خواسته here means 'with/at the (your) request' — خواسته is used nominally as 'requested/desired'.
segments.s361-g74: نگاران is a poetic plural noun referring to attractive young people / beloved ones (not painters here); با introduces the following descriptive phrase; جعد is used adjectivally as 'curly‑haired' and آراسته is a past‑participle/adjective meaning 'adorned' — together 'adorned with curls'.
segments.s361-g75: و is the connective 'and/then'; ایدونک is a temporal/conditional particle meaning roughly 'now/if at this time'; نپذیری is 2nd‑person negative present/subjunctive 'you do not accept' (literally 'you accept not'); این پند من = 'this advice of mine' (این 'this', پند 'advice', من as possessor).
segments.s361-g76: ببینی is 2nd‑person present/subjunctive used here with future/result sense 'you will see'; the noun phrase گران آهنین بند من places adjectives after the noun in classical order: گران 'heavy', آهنین 'iron(‑)', بند 'fetter/bond' and من 'my' as possessor — 'my heavy iron fetter(s)'.
segments.s361-g77: بیایم is a subjunctive/rhetorical 1st‑person form 'shall I come / may I come'; پس is an adverb 'then'; نامه is the direct noun 'a letter'; تا چندگاه forms a duration/interrogative phrase 'for how long' with تا marking extent/duration and چندگاه 'for a while/how long'.
segments.s361-g78: کنم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive 'I will cause / I will make'; کشورت را is the definite accusative 'your country' (کشورت = 'your country', را marks the object); سراسر is an adverb/adjective 'throughout/entire(ly)' and تباه is the predicate adjective 'ruined' — 'make your country utterly ruined.'
segments.s361-g79: سپاهی here denotes troops/warriors (an army); بیارم is the 1st‑person present/subjunctive of 'bring' (in the data the lemma senses given do not match, so sense is flagged); ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; ترکان چین reads as 'the Turks of Chin/China' (چین as a proper place name).
segments.s361-g80: که introduces a relative clause 'who/whose/that'; بنگاهشان (their بنگاه) is 'their onset/standing/assault' with the possessive suffix -شان (the provided sense list for بنگاه does not match the poetic sense, so it is flagged); بر نتابد is a negative present construction where نتابد 'cannot endure/withstand' has the preposition/particle بر preceding it; زمین 'the earth' is the grammatical subject placed after the verb in inverted poetic word order.
segments.s361-g33: برافگندی is a 2nd‑person past/preterite verb (you + past action) meaning 'you raised/established' (poetic diction). آیین شاهان خویش is the direct object: آیین (customs/rites) + شاهان (kings) + خویش ش (his/your own) — the possessive clitic ش attaches to خوی to mark 'your/his own'. Word order is verb then its object phrase, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s361-g34: بزرگان گیتی که بودند پیش is a relative clause modifying the preceding noun phrase: بزرگان گیتی ('the great ones/of the world') then که introduces the relative clause بودند پیش ('who were before'). بودند is past plural 'they were' and پیش functions adverbially as 'before/previously'.
segments.s361-g35: رها کردی is a compound past tense ('you let go / you abandoned') formed by the verb رها + the past auxiliary/verb کردی. آن پهلوی کیش را is the definite object marked by را: آن (that) + پهلوی (here adjectival, 'Pahlavi' / 'old/side') + کیش (creed/sect). The object marker را signals this whole phrase is the verb's object.
segments.s361-g36: چرا is the question word 'why'. ننگریدی is the negated past form 'you did not look/consider' (ن- prefix + نگر + -یدی). پس و پیش را is a frozen/binomial phrase 'the after and the before' (i.e. consequences, earlier and later matters) and functions here as the object of ننگریدی; را marks it as definite object.
segments.s361-g37: تو فرزند آنی که frames a relative clause: تو = 'you'; فرزند = 'son/child' (here 'son'); آنی که = 'that one who/whom' (آنی is a demonstrative-like form, followed by the relative particle که). The relative clause will continue in the next segment naming what the king did.
segments.s361-g38: بدو داد تاج از میان سپاه is the rest of the relative clause: فرخنده شاه (auspicious king) + بدو ('to him' = indirect object clitic phrase) + داد (gave) + تاج (the direct object 'crown') + از میان سپاه ('from among the host/army') as a prepositional phrase. Word order in poetry can separate subject and verb; here داد is the main past verb governing بدو and تاج.
segments.s361-g39: ورا برگزید از گزینان خویش: ورا ('him' / to him) is the object pronoun; برگزید is the past 'he chose/selected'; از گزینان خویش means 'from his chosen ones' — از + گزینان ('the chosen') and خویش + ش marks possession ('his own'). The clause means 'he chose him from his own selected (men)'.
segments.s361-g40: ز جمشیدیان مر ترا داشت پیش is parsed: ز جمشیدیان = 'from/among the Jamshidis'; مر = 'me' (first person oblique), ترا = 'you' (2nd person object + را), داشت = past 'he had/held', پیش = 'before/in front of'. Together it means 'among the Jamshidis he held you before me' i.e. he placed/esteemed you ahead of me.
segments.s361-g17: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; it introduces the sender. The following sequence ارجاسپ سالار گردان چین groups name + title + genitive: سالار modifies گردان (i.e. 'commander of the troop'), and چین specifies which troop — 'the Chinese troop.' Together: 'from Arjasp, commander of the Chinese host.'
segments.s361-g18: سوار is a noun 'rider/horseman' followed by the adjective جهان‌دیده 'world‑seen' meaning experienced/traveled; گرد زمین is an adverbial phrase 'around/about the earth' (گرد = 'around'). Together they describe the sender as 'a worldly horseman who traverses the earth.'
segments.s361-g19: نوشت is past third‑person 'he wrote.' اندر آن (spelled اندران) is a fused locative/adverb 'in that / therein' giving the location of the writing, and نامه خسروی = 'the royal letter' (خسروی adjectivally modifies نامه). The whole chunk means 'he wrote in that royal letter.'
segments.s361-g20: This is an instrumental/qualifying phrase describing the manner of the writing. نکو آفرینی is a laudatory compound ('good/excellent creation' → 'well‑crafted' or 'elegant'), خط is 'hand/handwriting,' and یبغوی functions adjectivally (here indicating the Yabghu‑style or association with Yabghu). Together: 'in an elegant hand (the Yabghu hand).' (The phrase characterizes the script/manner in which the letter was written.)
segments.s361-g21: که‌ای is a vocative/exclamatory 'O' (used to address), followed by نامور 'renowned' and شهریار 'sovereign/king'; جهان completes the title as 'sovereign of the world.' The order is vocative + epithet + title + domain.
segments.s361-g22: فروزنده is a present‑participle/adjective 'illuminating, radiant' modifying تاج 'crown.' شاهنشهان (plural/collective) means 'of the shahanshahs / emperors.' The phrase is a second epithet: 'illuminator of the emperors' crown' (i.e. a glorious ornament of imperial rule).
segments.s361-g23: سرت ('your head') is the theme; سبز باد is an optative formula where سبز = 'green/verdant' (a conventional wish for long life/prosperity) and باد is the optative particle 'may it be.' و connects تن و جان (body and life/soul) and درست predicates them ('be sound/whole'). The sequence is a set of wishes: 'May your head be green, and your body and soul be intact.'
segments.s361-g24: مبادت is a negative optative 'may it not be' (a wish that something not happen). کیانی qualifies کمرگاه (the Kayanian 'waist'/'girdle' — metaphorically the strength of the Kayanian line), and سست is 'weak.' The clause prays that the Kayanian backbone/strength never become weak.
segments.s361-g81: بینبارم is an inflected verb form with an implicit first-person subject ('I'); the verb appears before its noun phrase (verb‑initial order common in epic Persian). این رود جیحون is a noun phrase 'this river Jihun' (این = demonstrative + رود = noun + جیحون = proper name). به مشک here uses به in an instrumental/comitative sense 'with/filled with musk'.
segments.s361-g82: به مشک repeats the instrumental 'with musk'; آب دریا is a genitival noun phrase 'the sea's water' (noun + noun). کنم is first‑person present/subjunctive or optative 'I make / I will make' used here to express the speaker's intention; پاک خشک are two post‑nominal adjectives modifying آب دریا ('pure' and 'dry').
segments.s361-g83: بسوزم is a first‑person verb form 'I will burn / I burn' (verb before object). نگاریده is a past‑participle adjective 'painted / adorned' that precedes the noun کاخ 'palace'. ترا is the clitic object 'you/your' (ت = 2nd‑person suffix) together with را as the definite object marker, so نگاریده کاخ ترا را = 'your adorned palace (as object)'.
segments.s361-g84: ز بن is a prepositional phrase 'from the base / foundation' (ز = 'from' + بن = 'root/foundation'). برکنم is first‑person 'I will pluck up / uproot' (برکنم = to pull up/pluck off). بیخ و شاخ ترا ('your root and branch') is the coordinated object (بیخ = root, شاخ = branch), with ترا again showing the object belongs to 'you'; word order places the prepositional phrase before the verb.
segments.s361-g85: زمین را is the definite object 'the earth' (را marks it as definite/object). سراسر is an adverb/adjectival element 'throughout/entire', placed before the verb for emphasis. بسوزم is again first‑person 'I will burn', and همه reinforces totality ('all/entirely').
segments.s361-g86: کتفتان is a possessive/plural form meaning 'your shoulders' (کتف + -تان). به ناوک uses به as instrumental 'with/by (means of) spears/points'. بدوزم is literally 'I will sew' but here used figuratively/violently 'I will (fix/pierce) with spears' — a causative/action verb in first person. همه again quantifies 'all'.
segments.s361-g87: ز ایرانیان is a prepositional phrase 'from / among the Iranians'. هرچ is a quantifier 'however many / whatever', and پیر is the adjective 'old' modifying implied 'men' (مردست is obscure/archaic here and likely refers to 'men' or is an archaic copular/nominal form). The phrase sets up a generalization over Iranian men, including the old.
segments.s361-g88: کشان is a participial/agentive form 'those who drag / hauling' or a suffix forming an active verbal adjective; بنده کردن is the verbal phrase 'to make (someone) a slave' (کردن = to make/do). نباشد here expresses non‑existence or exclusion in context ('there will not be / will not be among them'); هژیر is the proper name 'Hojir'. The clause says that among those dragged off to be made slaves, Hojir will not be one.
segments.s361-g25: شنیدم is 1st-person singular past 'I heard'. که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'). راهی گرفتی is literally 'you took a road/way' (راهی as the object/route, followed by the past 2nd-person verb گرفتی). تباه is an adjective placed after the verb for poetic effect meaning 'ruinous/ruined' and qualifies the action or path.
segments.s361-g26: مرا (here split in the wordlist) is the 1st‑person object 'me' (م + را); را is the object marker. روز روشن is a noun phrase 'bright/clear day' (روز modified by روشن). بکردی is the past 2nd‑person 'you made' and سیاه is a predicate adjective 'black/dark', so the clause means 'you made my clear day black.'
segments.s361-g27: بیامد is the simple past 'came'. یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' (introducing someone). پیر مهتر is a noun phrase 'old chief/eldest leader' (پیر 'old' + مهتر 'chief/elder'); فریب here functions as a name or epithet ('Deceit'/'Deceiver') attached after the title, a common poetic device.
segments.s361-g28: ترا is the object 'to you' (in the wordlist split as T + را: ت is the 2nd‑person clitic and را the object marker). دل پر از بیم کرد is a causative construction: دل 'heart' + پر 'full (of)' + از بیم 'with/from fear' + کرد 'made/caused', so 'he made your heart full of fear'. و نهیب adds a coordinated noun 'and (gave) a rebuke'—نهیب is the noun 'a rebuke/harsh cry.'
segments.s361-g29: سخن گفتش is 'he spoke' or 'he said' with the clitic ش on گفت indicating 'to him/her' (indirect object). از دوزخ و از بهشت is a prepositional series 'about/from Hell and about/from Paradise'—از introduces the topics being spoken of.
segments.s361-g30: به دلت اندرون is a prepositional phrase 'into/to your heart within' (به + دلت with دلت = دل + ت second‑person clitic). هیچ شادی نهشت is an older/poetic clause meaning 'he placed no joy'—هیچ 'no/none', شادی 'joy', and نهشت is an archaic past form meaning roughly 'placed/laid' (here used negatively 'placed no joy').
segments.s361-g31: تو او را پذیرفتی is straightforward: تو 'you' (subject), او را 'him' (object with را), پذیرفتی past 2nd‑person 'you accepted/received'. و links the next object phrase: دینش را is 'his religion' (دین 'religion' + ش 'his' + را object marker) preparing for the following verb.
segments.s361-g32: بیاراستی is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning roughly 'you set straight/arranged/adorned' (here governing the objects that follow). راه و آیینش را are the coordinated direct objects 'his way/path and his ritual/rites' (آیینش = آیین + ش 'his'; را marks the definite objects). Word order places the verb before its objects in poetic diction but the meaning is 'you arranged/adopted his way and rites.'
segments.s361-g89: ازیشان (از + ایشان) is a prepositional phrase 'from them'. نیابی is a negative/conditional present/subjunctive form of 'یاب' meaning 'do not obtain / cannot get' and here introduces the conditional 'if you do not obtain'. فزونی بها is a noun phrase where فزونی ('increase, greater') qualifies بها ('price/value') = 'a greater price'. Word order is object (prepositional phrase) + verb + noun-phrase.
segments.s361-g90: کنمشان is the verb کنم 'I do/make' with the enclitic pronoun -شان 'them' = 'I will do to them / I will make them'. همه is 'all/everyone'. سر ز گردن جدا is a tightly bound phrase literally 'head from neck apart' = 'separate the heads from the necks' (i.e. behead). Here کنمشان expresses the speaker's intention/result of the preceding conditional ('I will ...').
segments.s361-g91: زن و کودکانشان = 'their wives (or women) and children'; شان is the possessive enclitic 'their' attached to the second noun but applying to both. بیارم is an archaic/poetic first-person verb form 'I bring / I will bring' (literally بیاورم), here meaning 'I will bring (forth)'. ز پیش literally 'from before' = 'before me / into my presence'. The phrase order places the objects before the verb, common in classical Persian.
segments.s361-g92: کنمشان again = 'I will make/do to them' with -شان 'them'. همه bende (همه بنده) = 'all (of them) servants/slaves'; شهر خویش is a genitive phrase meaning 'the city of my own' (خوی + enclitic ش = 'own / his'), i.e. 'my own city' — together: 'I will make them all slaves of my city.' The verb is first-person and declares the speaker's action upon the objects introduced earlier.
segments.s361-g93: زمینشان = 'their land/country' (possession marked by -شان). همه پاک is an intensifying pair 'entirely/utterly' (همه = 'all/entirely', پاک here adverbially 'completely'). ویران کنم is the verb phrase 'I will ruin / I will lay waste' (کنم = 'I make/do' combined with adjective/verb ویران). The whole means 'I will make their land completely desolate.'
segments.s361-g94: درختانش = 'their trees' (enclitic -ش). از بیخ و بن is an idiom 'from root and base' meaning 'from the very roots' or 'completely.' برکنم is first-person verb 'I will uproot / pluck up' (برکندن/برکنم). Word order places the object before the verb and the ablative phrase (از بیخ و بن) specifies the extent.
segments.s361-g95: بگفتم is 'I said' (perfect/past-type narrative). همه گفتنی = 'all that is sayable / everything worth saying' (گفتنی = 'sayable/noteworthy'). سر بسر is a poetic/idiomatic duplication used as an intensifier meaning 'throughout, completely, bit by bit / one by one' — here it emphasizes that the speaker has said everything. The whole clause: 'I have said all that should be said, completely.'
segments.s361-g96: تو is the vocative 'you'. ژرف 'deep/profound' modifies the following locative phrase. اندرین = 'in this / in these' (poetic form) and together ژرف اندرین پند نامه = 'deeply into this book of admonition/advice' (پند نامه = 'book of counsel'). نگر is the imperative 'look! / consider!' addressing the addressee to study it closely. Word order places the modifier and locative before the noun, with the imperative final.
segments.s361-g49: همی is an adverbial particle meaning 'continually / always' placed before the verb for an iterative or emphatic sense. تافتی is the 2nd-person singular past (poetic) form of تافتن 'to shine' (so: 'you shone/you shone continually'). بر جهان یکسره is a prepositional phrase ('on/upon the whole world'); یکسره ('completely / whole') modifies جهان and follows it in typical Persian word order.
segments.s361-g50: چو introduces a simile here ('like / as'). اردیبهشت آفتاب is a compound image 'the sun of Ordibehesht' (i.e. a spring sun). از marks origin or source ('from'), and بره literally 'a lamb'—a poetic image here (the sun is compared to or said to come from/shine as from a lamb).
segments.s361-g51: زگیتی ('زِ گیتی') = 'from the world' is a prepositional/compositional phrase placed up front. ترا is the 2nd-person object (ت + را, here fused) marking 'you' as the object. برگزیده is a past participle 'chosen' used as the verb, and خدای is the nominative subject 'God' (word order is inverted for poetic effect: participle + subject = 'God has chosen you').
segments.s361-g52: مهانت is a noun referring to the addressee's dignity/majesty (here used of the person's honour or lofty status). همه 'all' quantifies it. پیش is locative/'in the presence of' and همراه با بوده (the auxiliary/past participle 'has been') yields a perfective locative construction: 'has all been in the presence of / before'. به پای is an idiomatic locative phrase 'at the feet (of)' — so the whole clause means 'all your majesty has been at (before) His feet.'
segments.s361-g53: نکردی is the 2nd-person singular past negative of کردن 'to do' — here 'you did not do' = 'you did not give'. خدای جهان را is the definite object 'the God of the world' (را marks the definite object/recipient), and سپاس follows as the thing 'thanks' — together: 'you did not give thanks to the God of the world.'
segments.s361-g54: نبودی is the negative past/conditional form of بودن ('you would not / were not'); here it functions as a hypothetical/result clause 'you would not be'. بدین is the archaic fused form equivalent to به + این = 'in/by this', and ره 'way/course' follows: بدین ره = 'by/through this way'. ورا = 'to him' (vora, objective/benefactive particle = 'unto him'). حق‌شناس is an adjective/noun 'one who knows/rightly acknowledges' i.e. 'grateful' or 'truth-knower' placed after the verb phrase: 'you would not thereby be known as grateful to him.'
segments.s361-g55: ازان پس که = 'after that, when' (ازان = 'thereupon / from that', پس = 'after', که introduces the subordinate clause 'when/that'). ایزد ترا شاه کرد is the clause 'God made you king' — ایزد (subject) + ترا (2nd-person object) + شاه (object/predicative noun 'king') + کرد (past 'made'). Word order is subject–object–verb/predicative typical of older poetic style.
segments.s361-g56: یکی = 'a certain / one' introduces an indefinite person. پیر here is 'old / elderly' (an old man, in context an aged magician). جادوت is a poetic form of جادو ('magic' / 'enchantment') and functions with پیر to mean 'an old enchanter / sorcerer'. بی‌راه 'wrongly / astray' modifies the action, and کرد is the past verb 'he did / he made' — together: 'a certain old magician led (you) astray (with his magic)'.
segments.s361-g9: نوشتش is the past tense verb 'he wrote' with the clitic object -ش ('it'). به نام is a fixed prepositional phrase 'in the name of' where به governs نام; خدای جهان is a genitive construction 'God of the world' (خدای modified by جهان).
segments.s361-g10: شناسنده is an active participle/noun 'knower' used as an epithet of God; آشکار و نهان are coordinated adjectives 'manifest and hidden' that specify what God knows. There is no separate verb here—the whole phrase apposes or qualifies 'خدای جهان' from the previous segment.
segments.s361-g11: نوشتم is first-person past 'I wrote' (the suffix -م marks the subject). یکی نامه‌ای is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain letter' (نامه‌ای with the indefinite -ای); شهریار functions as the addressee/recipient ('to the king'), the preposition به is omitted as is common in poetic phrasing.
segments.s361-g12: چنانچون is a subordinating conjunction 'just as' or 'as when.' بد modifies خور (بد خور = 'ill-omened sun'), اندر is a locative/prepositional particle 'in' or 'upon,' and روزگار ('time/fate') completes the phrase: literally 'as when the ill sun is in the time (of fate),' i.e. when fate's sun turns unfavorable.
segments.s361-g13: سوی گرد is a prepositional phrase 'toward / to the presence of' (سوی = 'toward', گرد here indicating 'in the presence/court of'). گشتاسپ شاه زمین names the addressee: 'Gushtasp, king of the land/earth' (شاه followed by the epithet زمین).
segments.s361-g14: سزاوار is an adjective 'worthy/fit'; گاه کیان literally 'the time/seat of the Kays' (گاه used poetically for 'throne/seat'), and بافرین is an honorific 'praiseworthy / glorious' attached postpositively. The phrase means 'fit for the Kayan throne, praiseworthy.'
segments.s361-g15: گزین here works as an epithet 'chosen'; و links it to the next epithet. مهین پور is a compound epithet 'mighty/majestic son' (مهین 'majestic' + پور 'son' as a patronymic element), لهراسپ is the personal name, and شاه apposes the whole as 'king.' Together they form the title 'the chosen, majestic son Lohrasp, king.'
segments.s361-g16: خداوند here is an honorific 'lord/sovereign' applied to the king; جیش 'army' and نگهدار 'guardian/preserver' are two coordinated titles, and گاه (poetically) means 'the throne/seat.' The whole reads 'lord of the army and guardian of the throne.'
segments.s361-g41: بران سان که is a single connective phrase meaning 'so/in such a way that' (بران + سان + relative که). کیخسرو is the proper name and کینه‌جوی is a postpositive epithet ('the vengeful Ki‑Khosrow'); adjectives or participial epithets commonly follow the name in epic Persian.
segments.s361-g42: ترا here is the clitic you: ت is the 2nd‑person enclitic and را is the object/definite marker (together 'târâ' = 'you' as direct object, with emphasis). بیش بود is a comparative predicate ('there was more' / 'valued more'), where بود is the past copula. از introduces the standard of comparison ('than'); کیان آبروی = 'the Kians' honour' (کیان governs آبروی in the genitive sense).
segments.s361-g43: A triadic list joined by و (and). Each noun is a nominal quality: بزرگی 'greatness', شاهی 'kingship/regal status' (شاهی here means office/royalty), فرخندگی 'good fortune/felicity'. The simple noun + و pattern is a standard list construction in Persian.
segments.s361-g44: A continuation of the qualities, again linked by و: توانایی 'ability/capability', فر is the technical Shahnameh term for royal/divine glory (farr), and زیبندگی 'beauty/ornament'. فر is a loaded cultural term and functions here as a noun in the list.
segments.s361-g45: درفشان و پیلان آراسته is a pictorial phrase: درفشان (literally 'standard‑bearers' or 'banners waving') and پیلان (plural of پیل, 'elephants') followed by the postpositive adjective آراسته 'adorned'. In epic style adjectives often follow the nouns they modify.
segments.s361-g46: A second list of abundance: بسی لشکر 'many / numerous hosts' (بسی is an archaic quantifier here), و گنج 'and treasure', و بس خواسته — بس here is an intensifier ('much/abundant') and خواسته functions as a past‑participle/noun 'desires/wishes' ('many wishes' or 'many things sought'). The sequence is nominal and descriptive, not a finite clause.
segments.s361-g47: همی بودت is an archaic/homeric‑style past form: همی adds continuous/habitual sense ('ever/always') and بودت is the past 2nd‑person copula 'you were'. ای is the vocative 'O', and مهتر شهریار is an honorific address 'foremost/ chief sovereign'—two nouns in apposition used to address the addressee.
segments.s361-g48: همه مهتران is the subject 'all the chiefs' (epic plural). مر is an archaic dative/experiential particle 'to me' (marking the speaker's viewpoint), ترا (ت + را) is the emphatic object 'you', and دوستدار is a predicative adjective/participle 'fond of' or 'admirers of' — together: 'all the chiefs, in my view, were fond of you.'
segments.s361-g1: برین is the prepositional phrase 'بر' + the demonstrative/orthographic ین (here equivalent to این), literally 'on/against these'; it precedes the verb as is common in older Persian. ایستادند is 3rd-person plural past 'they stood/took a stand'. ترکان چین is a noun phrase 'the Turks of Chin/China' (ترکان = Turks, چین = China) with the place/name following the group it qualifies.
segments.s361-g2: دو تن is a numeral + classifier meaning 'two persons/men'. نیز is the adverb 'also'. کردند is the past plural 'they did/made' used here as the light verb combining with the following verbal stem. زیشان is a compound of زِ + شان meaning 'from them / of them'. گزین is the bare stem 'choose/select' functioning as the complement of کردند (a common poetic/archaic pattern: کردند + stem = 'they chose').
segments.s361-g3: یکی introduces an indefinite/particular individual 'one (of them) / a certain one'. نام او is the possessive construction 'his name', followed by the name in apposition. بیدرفش is a compound proper name (here used as the personal name) and بزرگ is an epithet 'the Great' that follows the name in apposition (typical Persian word order: name + epithet).
segments.s361-g4: This string gives further appositive epithets. گوی here functions as a noun/epithet related to 'uttering/saying' (literary use 'the one called...'); پیر is 'old/elder' as an epithet. و simply links epithets. جادو is a noun 'magic / sorcery' used adjectivally to mark him as a magician. ستنبه سترگ is a two‑word descriptive phrase: ستنبه 'warning/admonition' and سترگ 'sight/eye' (poetically 'a great thing to the eye' or 'a formidable spectacle'). The ordering (epithets following the name) is typical in epic Persian.
segments.s361-g5: دگر means 'the other' (introducing the second man). جادوی here is the adjectival/attributive form 'magical' used as a name or title ('the magician' / 'Jādūy'). نام او نام خواست is slightly elliptical: the first نام is the direct object 'a name' (i.e. 'renown, a name'), and خواست is the past 'he wanted'; together idiomatically 'he sought a name' = 'he sought fame' or 'he wished to make a name for himself.' Grammatically this is object (نام) + pronoun possession (او) + verbal predicate (نام خواست).
segments.s361-g6: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'for/that'. هرگز is the negative adverb 'never'. دلش is 'his heart' (دل + possessive ش = his). جز is the preposition 'except'. تباهی is the noun 'destruction/ruin'. نخواست is the past negative 'did not want' (here idiomatically 'his heart desired nothing except ruin'). The clause gives the motive/character: he only desired ruin.
segments.s361-g7: یکی again introduces 'one (of them)'. نامه بنوشت is the verbal clause 'wrote a letter'; بنوشت is an archaic/past form of 'wrote' in epic style (here parsed as the past singular verb). خوب و هژیر are appositive descriptors: خوب 'good/valiant' qualifies هژیر (a personal name, Hojir) — in poetry adjectives and names often appear side by side without extra function words.
segments.s361-g8: سوی is the preposition 'toward / to' marking the addressee. نامور خسرو is the appositive 'the famed Khosrow' (نامور before the name as an epithet). و links the next epithet; دین پذیر literally 'religion‑accepting' is an epithet meaning 'pious' or 'faithful' placed after the name. The whole phrase is the indirect object ('to the famed, pious Khosrow').

sentences.s361-s29: When your news reached me, on my bright day a star appeared.
sentences.s361-s30: I wrote a friendly letter, for we were both friends and good companions.
sentences.s361-s31: When you read the letter, wash your head and body; do not show me the deceiver's face either.
sentences.s361-s32: Unbind Maran's band from the middle; begin the brightening with joy.
sentences.s361-s33: Accept my counsel, O one who utters a little cry — from the Turks no harm will come to you either.
sentences.s361-s34: May the land‑seizers and the Turks of China be, for you, like the land of Iran.
sentences.s361-s35: I will give you these boundless treasures which I have brought together with hardships.
sentences.s361-s36: Well‑colored horses, with silver and gold; he has set gems in their stirrups.
sentences.s361-s37: I have sent servants to you, as you asked, lovely youths adorned with curling locks.
sentences.s361-s38: And if you now do not accept this counsel of mine, you will see my heavy iron bonds.
sentences.s361-s39: Shall I then come with a letter — for how long will I make your country entirely ruined?
sentences.s361-s40: I will bring soldiers from the Turks of Chin, whose onset the earth cannot endure.
sentences.s361-s17: You set up the rites of your kings and the great ones of the world who were before.
sentences.s361-s18: You abandoned that Pahlavi creed; why did you not look to the before and after?
sentences.s361-s19: You are the son of that one whom the auspicious king gave the crown to from among the host.
sentences.s361-s20: He chose him from his elect; among the Jamshidis he set you before me.
sentences.s361-s9: From Arjasp, commander of the Chinese host, a worldly horseman who traverses the earth.
sentences.s361-s10: He wrote in that royal letter, in a fine/apt hand of Yabghu.
sentences.s361-s11: O renowned sovereign of the world, illuminator of the crown of emperors.
sentences.s361-s12: May your head be green, and may your body and life be sound; may the Kayanian waist/girdle not be weak.
sentences.s361-s41: I will fill this Jihun river with musk; with musk I will make the sea's water pure and dry.
sentences.s361-s42: I will burn your adorned palace; from its foundation I will uproot your root and branch.
sentences.s361-s43: I will burn the whole earth through and through; I will pierce all your shoulders with spear‑points.
sentences.s361-s44: Among the Iranians — whatever men there are, even the old — those whom they haul off to make slaves will not include Hojir.
sentences.s361-s13: I heard that you took to the road and ruined it; you turned my clear day black.
sentences.s361-s14: There came a certain old chief, Deceit; he filled your heart with fear and (gave) a rebuke.
sentences.s361-s15: He spoke to him of Hell and of Paradise; into your innermost heart he placed no joy.
sentences.s361-s16: You received him and adopted his religion; you set his path and rites in order.
sentences.s361-s45: If you cannot obtain any higher price from them, I will sever all their heads from their necks.
sentences.s361-s46: I will bring their wives and children before me and make them all slaves of my city.
sentences.s361-s47: I will utterly lay waste to their land, and uproot their trees from root to base.
sentences.s361-s48: I have said all that can be said, in full; you, look deeply into this book of counsel.
sentences.s361-s25: You continually shone over the whole world like the spring sun from a lamb.
sentences.s361-s26: God has chosen you from the world; your majesty (all your dignity) has been before His feet.
sentences.s361-s27: You did not thank the God of the world; by this road you would not be counted grateful to Him.
sentences.s361-s28: After that, when God made you king, a certain old sorcerer led you astray with his magic.
sentences.s361-s5: He wrote it in the name of God, the knower of the world, manifest and hidden.
sentences.s361-s6: I wrote a certain letter to the king, just as the ill-omened sun of fate appears.
sentences.s361-s7: To Gushtasp, king of the earth, worthy of the Kayan throne and praiseworthy.
sentences.s361-s8: The chosen, the mighty son Lohrasp, king — lord of the host and guardian of the throne.
sentences.s361-s21: So much so that Ki-Khosrow, the vengeful, valued you more than the Kian dynasty's honour.
sentences.s361-s22: Greatness and kingship and good fortune; ability and farr (royal glory) and beauty.
sentences.s361-s23: With banners aloft and adorned elephants; with numerous hosts and treasure and many desires.
sentences.s361-s24: You were ever, O foremost sovereign; all the chiefs, in my view, were your admirers.
sentences.s361-s1: The Turks of Chin took their stand against them; they also chose two men from among them.
sentences.s361-s2: One — his name was Bīdar‑fash the Great — an elder called Gūy and a magician, a formidable sight to behold.
sentences.s361-s3: The other — called Jādū (the magician) — sought a name (sought fame), for his heart never wished anything but ruin.
sentences.s361-s4: One wrote a letter — Hojir the valiant — to the famed Khosrow, the devout.
